title: Liver and onions
subtitle: Calves' or lambs' liver with slow-cooked onion gravy
excerpt: Thinly sliced liver cooked briefly in a hot pan and served on a bed of deeply caramelised onion gravy. The British offal dish that repays good timing more than any other.
sourceNotes: Liver and onions is a staple of the British and Irish household cooking tradition, documented in Isabella Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and a consistent presence in twentieth-century British cookery books including Delia Smith's Complete Cookery Course (1978-82).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The onion gravy can be made a day ahead and reheated. Cook the liver fresh to order.

content.text: Liver and onions is one of those dishes where execution matters more than ingredients. The liver must be sliced thin and cooked very briefly: 2 minutes on each side over high heat, no more. Liver cooked beyond this point turns grey, grainy, and strongly flavoured in a way that puts people off for life. Properly cooked liver is pink inside, tender, and mild enough to eat with pleasure even by those who think they dislike it.

Calves' liver is the finest and most expensive variety; lambs' liver is a good alternative with a slightly stronger flavour. Both work well here. The onion gravy takes about 25 minutes and can be made in advance; the liver takes 4 minutes and must be cooked to order.

items.prepNote: or lambs' liver, sliced 5 mm thick

content.text: Melt half the butter in a wide frying pan over medium-low heat. Add {{onion}} finely sliced onion with a pinch of salt and cook slowly for 20 to 25 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deeply golden and sweet. Sprinkle over {{plain-flour}} of flour and stir for 1 minute. Add {{stock-beef}} of beef stock and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce. Cook, stirring, for 5 minutes until thickened into a gravy. Add the thyme if using. Season and keep warm on low heat. Remove and discard the thyme before serving.

Season {{calves-liver}} of liver slices with salt and pepper just before cooking. Heat the remaining butter in a clean frying pan over high heat until foaming. If using sage, fry the leaves for 30 seconds until crisp, then set aside. Lay the liver slices in the pan in a single layer and cook for 2 minutes on each side for slices 5 mm thick. The liver is done when the edges are brown but the centre is still slightly pink when pressed. Do not cook further.
Spoon the onion gravy onto warmed plates and lay the liver slices on top. Top with fried sage if using. Serve with mashed potato.

items.symptom: Liver is grey, grainy, and strongly flavoured
items.fix: Liver must be eaten pink inside. Once grey throughout it is ruined — no amount of sauce will fix the texture or flavour. Next time, watch the clock: 2 minutes per side maximum.

items.symptom: Liver is still bloody in the centre
items.cause: Slices were too thick, or the pan was not hot enough
items.fix: Slice to a consistent 5 mm and ensure the pan is very hot before the liver goes in. A 5 mm slice cooks through at 2 minutes per side.

items.symptom: Onions have not caramelised after 20 minutes
items.cause: Pan was too cool or the onions were in a pile rather than spread out
items.fix: Spread the onions in a wide pan and be patient. They need low, slow heat and occasional stirring. High heat burns the outside before the inside sweetens.

items.symptom: Liver is tough and hard to cut
items.cause: Overcooked or the liver was not sliced thin enough
items.fix: Ask your butcher to slice it at 5 mm. The thinner the slice, the shorter the cooking time and the more tender the result.

content.text: Liver and onions is one of the most persistently misunderstood dishes in the British repertoire: widely remembered badly because it was almost invariably overcooked in school and institutional cooking, and almost invariably excellent when cooked properly. Its place in British domestic cooking goes back centuries; liver was inexpensive, nutritious, and available everywhere, and the pairing with slowly caramelised onions is one of the oldest flavour combinations in European cooking. The recent revival of nose-to-tail cooking has rehabilitated it in restaurant kitchens, where it appears as a straightforward, quick-cooking dish with excellent flavour and low cost.
